title: Practice Set 8 — Multi-Tenant Cluster Governance
description: Design NordGrid’s multi-tenant governance: diagnose a noisy-neighbor incident, design resource pools with preemption, evaluate cluster isolation models, implement cost allocation and chargeback, and produce a comprehensive governance policy.
version: authored 2026-04-06, targets PySpark 3.5+ / YARN Fair Scheduler

datasets.description: 240 rows: daily usage per team for 30 days. Contains March 15-17 ML spike causing 15 SLA violations on analytics-no and ingestion-fi.

tasks.title: Review NordGrid’s Current Cluster Sharing Model

description.context: NordGrid’s 8 teams share one production cluster with no resource quotas, no priority queues, and no cost visibility. On March 15-17, the ml-platform team ran a large training job that consumed 3.5x its normal resources. During those 3 days, analytics-no and ingestion-fi experienced all 15 of the month’s SLA violations.
description.task: Examine the usage data. Identify the 5 problems with the current sharing model, rank them by severity, and explain how each enables the next.
description.validationHint: The noisy-neighbor incident (ml-platform spike → analytics/ingestion starved) is the symptom. The root causes are structural: no quotas, no priority, no isolation.

incidentSummary.label: March 15-17 noisy-neighbor incident
incidentSummary.content:
ml-platform normal CPU: ~65 cpu-hours/day
ml-platform during incident: ~228 cpu-hours/day (3.5x spike)
Total cluster capacity: ~400 cpu-hours/day
ml-platform consumed 57% of cluster during incident

Impact:
  analytics-no: 2-5 SLA violations per day (Gold tables delivered late)
  ingestion-fi: 1-3 SLA violations per day (Finland Bronze delayed)
  Total: 15 SLA violations in 3 days
  All other days in March: 0 SLA violations

structuralProblems.label: Structural problems identified by platform team
structuralProblems.content:
1. No resource quotas: any team can consume unlimited cluster resources
2. No priority system: ml-platform (SLA=low) jobs run at same priority as ingestion-no (SLA=critical)
3. No cost visibility: teams don’t know their consumption or cost
4. No preemption: low-priority jobs cannot be paused for high-priority work
5. No isolation: one team’s spike directly starves all other teams

fields.label: Rank the 5 structural problems by severity (most severe first):
options: No priority system — SLA=low jobs block SLA=critical, directly causing violations
No resource quotas — enables any team to consume unlimited resources
No preemption — cannot reclaim resources from low-priority jobs during spikes
No isolation — one team’s spike starves all others
No cost visibility — teams have no incentive to optimize
correctOrder: No priority system — SLA=low jobs block SLA=critical, directly causing violations
No resource quotas — enables any team to consume unlimited resources
No preemption — cannot reclaim resources from low-priority jobs during spikes
No isolation — one team’s spike starves all others
No cost visibility — teams have no incentive to optimize
fields.rationale: Priority is most severe because the SLA inversion (low-priority ML blocking critical ingestion) directly caused all 15 violations. Quotas rank second as the structural enabler. Preemption ranks third as the missing recovery mechanism. Isolation and cost visibility are enabling factors.

fields.label: Why is the priority inversion (SLA=low blocking SLA=critical) the root cause?
options: Because ml-platform used too much CPU

Because without priorities, the scheduler treats ml-platform’s training job and ingestion-no’s Bronze pipeline as equal. When resources are scarce, both wait equally. But ingestion-no has a 30-minute SLA while ml-platform has no time constraint. The scheduler should preempt ML to serve ingestion.
Because analytics-no has too many pipelines
Because the cluster is too small for 8 teams
fields.correctAnswer: Because without priorities, the scheduler treats ml-platform’s training job and ingestion-no’s Bronze pipeline as equal. When resources are scarce, both wait equally. But ingestion-no has a 30-minute SLA while ml-platform has no time constraint. The scheduler should preempt ML to serve ingestion.

tasks.title: Design Resource Pool Allocation for 8 Teams

description.context: NordGrid’s platform team will implement YARN Fair Scheduler resource pools. Each pool has a guaranteed minimum share, a maximum cap, scheduling weight, and preemption policy. The design must prevent the March 15-17 incident while allowing flexible resource sharing during normal operations.
description.task: Design the resource pool configuration as a DataFrame. Each row defines one pool with its guaranteed minimum, maximum cap, weight, preemption policy, and the teams assigned to it. The pools must reflect SLA tiers: critical pipelines get guaranteed resources; low-priority jobs get elastic capacity.
description.validationHint: Pools don’t need to map 1:1 to teams. Group teams by SLA tier: critical pool (ingestion + platform), high pool (transform), standard pool (analytics + Finland), ML pool (ml-platform). Each pool’s minimum guarantee sums to ≤100% of cluster.

evidence.content:
Cluster total: 400 cpu-hours/day, 120 executor slots

Team SLA tiers:
  Critical: platform-core, ingestion-no (must complete within 30 min)
  High: transform-no, ingestion-se, transform-se (within 60 min)
  Standard: analytics-no, ingestion-fi (within 120 min)
  Low: ml-platform (no time constraint)

Fair Scheduler parameters per pool:
  minShare: guaranteed minimum (executors always available)
  maxShare: cap (cannot exceed, even if cluster is idle)
  weight: scheduling priority when competing for resources
  preemption: can this pool preempt others when under minimum?

fields.label: Should ml-platform have a maxShare cap?
options: No cap — ML should use all available resources when idle

Yes — cap ml-platform at 40% of cluster (48 executor slots). Without a cap, ML’s next training spike will again consume 57% of the cluster. The cap ensures 60% remains available for other teams even during ML peaks.
Cap at 10% — minimize ML’s impact
Cap only during business hours (06:00-18:00)
fields.correctAnswer: Yes — cap ml-platform at 40% of cluster (48 executor slots). Without a cap, ML’s next training spike will again consume 57% of the cluster. The cap ensures 60% remains available for other teams even during ML peaks.

starterScaffold.content:
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import col, lit

spark = SparkSession.builder.appName('nordgrid-resource-pools').master('local[*]').getOrCreate()

# YOUR SOLUTION BELOW
# Design resource pools as a DataFrame.
# Each row = one resource pool.
# Required columns: pool_name, sla_tier, assigned_teams,
#   min_share_pct, max_share_pct, weight, preemption_enabled,
#   rationale
# Constraints: sum of min_share_pct <= 100
# Minimum 4 pools (one per SLA tier).
# Store in 'resource_pools'



# YOUR SOLUTION ABOVE

# Verification
print('Resource Pool Configuration:')
resource_pools.show(truncate=False)
total_min = resource_pools.agg({'min_share_pct': 'sum'}).collect()[0][0]
print(f'Total min_share: {total_min}% (must be <= 100%)')

assertions.check: all(c in resource_pools.columns for c in ['pool_name', 'sla_tier', 'min_share_pct', 'max_share_pct', 'weight'])

assertions.description: ML pool has a max cap

description.context: After the noisy-neighbor incident, some team leads want dedicated clusters. The platform team evaluates three isolation models: shared cluster with resource pools (Task 2), cluster-per-country, and cluster-per-SLA-tier.
description.task: Evaluate the three models against NordGrid’s constraints: cost, isolation strength, resource efficiency, and operational complexity.
description.validationHint: More clusters = better isolation but worse efficiency (idle capacity can’t be shared). The sweet spot depends on how much isolation the organization actually needs.

evidence.content:
Option A: Shared Cluster + Resource Pools (from Task 2)
  Clusters: 1
  Cost: $15,600/month (current)
  Isolation: soft (pools can be exceeded if others are idle)
  Efficiency: ~85% (resources shared dynamically)
  Complexity: low (one cluster to manage)

Option B: Cluster-per-Country (Norway, Sweden, Finland)
  Clusters: 3
  Cost: $22,000/month (+41%)
  Isolation: hard (country data stays in country)
  Efficiency: ~55% (Finland cluster 30% utilized, can’t share with Norway)
  Complexity: medium (3 clusters, 3 configs)

Option C: Cluster-per-SLA-Tier (critical+high, standard, ML)
  Clusters: 3
  Cost: $19,500/month (+25%)
  Isolation: hard by SLA (ML can never affect critical)
  Efficiency: ~65% (ML cluster bursty, often idle)
  Complexity: medium (3 clusters, but pipelines may need cross-cluster data access)

fields.label: Which model do you recommend?
options: Option A (Shared + Pools) — lowest cost, highest efficiency. Resource pools with preemption prevent the noisy-neighbor incident without the overhead of multiple clusters.
Option B (per-country) — best for data sovereignty but 41% more expensive
Option C (per-SLA-tier) — guarantees ML can never affect critical, but 25% more expensive
Option A for Norway+Sweden, separate cluster for Finland (data sovereignty). 2 clusters total: shared pool for Norway+Sweden, dedicated Finland cluster.
fields.correctAnswer: Option A for Norway+Sweden, separate cluster for Finland (data sovereignty). 2 clusters total: shared pool for Norway+Sweden, dedicated Finland cluster.
fields.secondaryCorrectAnswer: Option A (Shared + Pools) — lowest cost, highest efficiency. Resource pools with preemption prevent the noisy-neighbor incident without the overhead of multiple clusters.
fields.distractorRationale: The 2-cluster hybrid addresses Finland’s sovereignty constraint (hard requirement from PX4) while keeping the cost-efficient shared model for Norway+Sweden. Pure Option A ignores sovereignty. Pure Option B wastes 45% of Finland’s cluster. The hybrid is the pragmatic compromise.

fields.label: What is the main risk of shared clusters with resource pools (Option A)?
options: Pools don’t actually limit resource usage

Soft isolation: if pool configs are misconfigured or preemption is delayed, a noisy neighbor can still cause transient SLA violations before preemption kicks in. Hard isolation (separate clusters) eliminates this risk entirely.
Shared clusters are always slower than dedicated
Resource pools add too much scheduling overhead
fields.correctAnswer: Soft isolation: if pool configs are misconfigured or preemption is delayed, a noisy neighbor can still cause transient SLA violations before preemption kicks in. Hard isolation (separate clusters) eliminates this risk entirely.

fields.label: When should NordGrid upgrade from shared+pools to separate clusters?
options: Immediately — separate clusters are always better

When the cost of SLA violations exceeds the cost of separate clusters. If pool-based isolation still produces 5+ violations/month ($4K+ in engineer time + business impact), the $4-6K/month premium for separate clusters is justified.
Never — shared is always sufficient with proper pools
When the cluster runs out of capacity
fields.correctAnswer: When the cost of SLA violations exceeds the cost of separate clusters. If pool-based isolation still produces 5+ violations/month ($4K+ in engineer time + business impact), the $4-6K/month premium for separate clusters is justified.

tasks.title: Design Cost Allocation and Chargeback Model

description.context: NordGrid’s finance team wants a monthly chargeback report: each team sees their compute cost and how it compares to their budget. The cost model must be fair (based on actual usage), transparent (teams can verify), and actionable (teams can reduce their cost by optimizing). The platform team must implement the model using the usage data.
description.task: Implement the cost allocation model: compute each team’s monthly cost from their CPU-hours and memory-GB-hours, add a shared overhead component, and produce a chargeback report. Include a budget comparison showing which teams are over/under budget.
description.validationHint: Cost = (cpu_hours × $0.10) + (memory_gb_hours × $0.02) + overhead_share. Overhead = 20% of total allocated equally. Budget comparison: team annual budget / 12 = monthly budget.

evidence.content:
Pricing:
  CPU: $0.10 per cpu-hour
  Memory: $0.02 per GB-hour
  Storage: $0.05 per TB-month
  Overhead: 20% of total direct cost, split equally across 8 teams

Team annual budgets:
  platform-core: $85,000  |  ingestion-no: $42,000
  transform-no: $55,000   |  analytics-no: $28,000
  ingestion-se: $38,000   |  transform-se: $45,000
  ingestion-fi: $22,000   |  ml-platform: $65,000

fields.label: Why should cost allocation include CPU-hours AND memory-GB-hours (not just one)?
options: CPU-hours alone is sufficient — memory correlates with CPU

Different workloads have different CPU/memory ratios. ML training is memory-heavy (32 GB executor, low CPU). ETL is CPU-heavy (8 GB, high CPU). Charging only CPU would under-charge ML and over-charge ETL. Both dimensions ensure fairness.
Memory is free — only CPU should be charged
Neither dimension matters — charge per pipeline run
fields.correctAnswer: Different workloads have different CPU/memory ratios. ML training is memory-heavy (32 GB executor, low CPU). ETL is CPU-heavy (8 GB, high CPU). Charging only CPU would under-charge ML and over-charge ETL. Both dimensions ensure fairness.

starterScaffold.content:
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import col, sum as spark_sum, round as spark_round, lit

spark = SparkSession.builder.appName('nordgrid-chargeback').master('local[*]').getOrCreate()

df_usage = spark.read.csv('PX8_data_team_usage.csv', header=True, inferSchema=True)

# Team budgets (annual)
budgets = {'platform-core': 85000, 'ingestion-no': 42000, 'transform-no': 55000,
           'analytics-no': 28000, 'ingestion-se': 38000, 'transform-se': 45000,
           'ingestion-fi': 22000, 'ml-platform': 65000}

# YOUR SOLUTION BELOW
# 1. Aggregate usage per team for the month
# 2. Compute direct_cost = cpu_hours*0.10 + memory_gb_hours*0.02 + storage_tb*0.05
# 3. Compute total direct cost, then overhead_share = total*0.20/8
# 4. total_cost = direct_cost + overhead_share
# 5. Add monthly_budget = annual_budget / 12
# 6. Add budget_status: 'over' if total_cost > monthly_budget, else 'under'
# Store in 'chargeback'



# YOUR SOLUTION ABOVE

# Verification
print('Monthly Chargeback Report:')
chargeback.orderBy(col('total_cost').desc()).show()
over = chargeback.filter(col('budget_status') == 'over').count()
print(f'Teams over budget: {over}')

assertions.check: all(c in chargeback.columns for c in ['team_name', 'direct_cost', 'total_cost', 'budget_status'])

assertions.description: ml-platform has highest direct cost (due to spike)
Budget status column exists with over/under values

description.context: NordGrid’s CTO has approved resource pools (Task 2) and chargeback (Task 4). The platform team must now produce the complete multi-tenant governance policy: resource pools, quotas, priority rules, cost allocation, SLA monitoring, escalation process, and capacity review cadence.
description.task: Produce the governance policy as a structured DataFrame. Each row is one policy rule. The rules must form a coherent system: resource pools enforce isolation, chargeback provides incentives, SLA monitoring detects violations, and the escalation process resolves incidents.
description.validationHint: The policy must prevent another March 15-17 incident. Test by tracing the scenario: if ml-platform spikes again, does the policy (1) cap ML at its max share, (2) preempt ML for critical jobs, (3) alert on-call, and (4) charge ML for the excess?

evidence.content:
Approved from Tasks 1-4:
- Resource pools by SLA tier with preemption
- ML capped at 40% of cluster
- Critical pool gets preemption rights
- Chargeback based on CPU-hours + memory-GB-hours + storage + overhead
- Finland on separate cluster (sovereignty)
- Cost visibility via monthly reports

fields.label: What happens when ml-platform exceeds its 40% cap?
options: ML jobs are immediately killed

ML jobs are queued until resources become available within the 40% cap. If critical/high pools need resources, ML jobs above the cap are preempted (gracefully terminated with checkpoint) to free resources. ML is charged for all consumed resources including the excess.
Nothing — the cap is advisory only
ML is billed at 2x rate for excess usage
fields.correctAnswer: ML jobs are queued until resources become available within the 40% cap. If critical/high pools need resources, ML jobs above the cap are preempted (gracefully terminated with checkpoint) to free resources. ML is charged for all consumed resources including the excess.

fields.label: How often should the platform team review capacity and pool allocations?
options: Only after incidents

Quarterly capacity review: examine 3 months of usage data, adjust pool min/max shares based on actual demand trends, review chargeback accuracy, and plan for growth. Annual budget review aligns pool sizes with team budgets.
Daily — continuous optimization
Never — set once and forget
fields.correctAnswer: Quarterly capacity review: examine 3 months of usage data, adjust pool min/max shares based on actual demand trends, review chargeback accuracy, and plan for growth. Annual budget review aligns pool sizes with team budgets.

starterScaffold.content:
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import col, lit

spark = SparkSession.builder.appName('nordgrid-governance').master('local[*]').getOrCreate()

# YOUR SOLUTION BELOW
# Produce multi-tenant governance policy as DataFrame.
# Each row = one policy rule.
# Required columns: rule_id, category, rule_text,
#   enforcement_method, responsible_team, review_frequency
# Categories: resource_pools, quotas, priority, cost_allocation,
#   sla_monitoring, escalation, capacity_review
# Minimum 10 rules.
# Store in 'governance'



# YOUR SOLUTION ABOVE

# Verification
print('Multi-Tenant Governance Policy:')
governance.show(truncate=False)
print(f'Total rules: {governance.count()}')
print(f'Categories: {[r.category for r in governance.select("category").distinct().collect()]}')

assertions.check: all(c in governance.columns for c in ['rule_id', 'category', 'rule_text', 'enforcement_method'])

conceptsCombined: Noisy-neighbor diagnosis and structural root causes (Task 1, Lessons 1-2)
Resource pool design with min/max/weight/preemption (Task 2, Lessons 3-4)
Cluster isolation models and cost-benefit (Task 3, Lessons 5-6, PX1)
Cost allocation and chargeback (Task 4, Lessons 7-8, PX1)
SLA monitoring and escalation process (Lessons 9-10, PM6-7)
synthesisNotes.rationale: This capstone produces NordGrid’s complete multi-tenant governance policy. The student must design rules that form a coherent system: pools enforce isolation, chargeback provides incentives, monitoring detects violations, and escalation resolves incidents. The litmus test: trace the March 15-17 incident through the policy and verify that every failure point is now addressed.

selfCheck.scaleMatters: 8 teams competing for one cluster. Governance, pools, and chargeback are meaningless for a single team.
selfCheck.theoryPracticeBoundary: Theory teaches resource management concepts. Practice requires designing a governance system that prevents a specific historical incident and creating operational policies other teams follow.
